sentence: Subjective:
HPI: Kayla Ward, a 14-year-old female, presents with a 2-year history of acne on the face, which is present most days, with no associated symptoms such as itching, bleeding, or pain.
She has been using over-the-counter items like washes and Perce Gel, which have been somewhat helpful.

She lives with her parents and sister and has a regular skin care routine that includes washing her face in the morning with just water and a washcloth, using an L'Oréal facial cleanser at night, a toner, and Perce Gel.
She uses sunscreen with SPF 30.
She has been getting regular periods for about 2 years and does not experience acne flares with her periods.
Past medical history: No additional past medical history.
Review of systems: No additional symptoms in other organs.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]

Physical exam: The examination revealed multiple benign moles on the trunk and extremities, scattered skin-colored papules, open and closed comedones, and erythematous papules on the face, primarily on the forehead and central cheeks and chin.
The chest and back are relatively spared.
The remainder of the examination is normal.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has mild to moderate acne, mostly comedonal with a small inflammatory component.
Problem list: Acne; [Status: active]
Plan:
I prescribed Adapalene 0.1% cream to be applied in a thin layer to the areas where she generally gets acne, starting with a few nights a week and gradually increasing to every night.
If the Adapalene is not covered by insurance or is too expensive, I recommended trying Differin Gel over the counter.
I also advised her to use a non-comedogenic moisturizer with sunscreen SPF 30 or higher, and to wear sunscreen when outside.
I cautioned her that the topical retinoid may cause some dryness or irritation.
Follow-up: We will see her in 6 months to check for progress.
